text:
Admission Date:  2002-03-02              Discharge Date:   1980-04-30

Date of Birth:  1975-02-16             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Kelly
Chief Complaint:
Portal vein thrombosis

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Colonoscopy


History of Present Illness:
72y/o F presented to ED after seeing PCP for weight loss,
fevers, and malaise over 3-4 weeks.  Pt notes that she lost
about 6# during this time.  Has had anorexia with decreased po
intake.  Fevers daily up to 102 for about one week.  No night
sweats.  Has noted darker urine during this time; stool not
acholic.  No BRBPR, no diarrhea, no hematemesis.  Denies frank
abdominal pain.  + nausea, occasional dry heaves.  Denies
myalgias.  Pt's daughter commented on change in color of skin -
"more pale" - but pt's vision blurry and cannot comment.
Fatigue has limited her level of activity - describes herself as
a baseline active person, has been lying in bed for the most
part for the last few weeks.  + SOB particularly with exertion.
Denies chest pressure or palpitations.
.
Pt has no known liver disease, no h/o EtOH.  Has not traveled
recently, never lived outside of this area.  No sick contacts.
.
No recent infectious symptoms - minimal headache, no back pain;
+slight nonproductive cough, no dysuria, no diarrhea, no
abdominal pain.
.
Pt noted to have RUQ tenderness found to have anemia and
leukocytosis with shift.  Portal vein thrombosis found on RUQ
ultrasound.
.
In the Carly Lucero, pt was given acetaminophen 1000mg, levofloxacin
500mg, metronidazole 500mg, vancomycin 1g, dolasetron 12.5mg,
ibuprofen 600mg.  Pt's BP dropped into 70s, and a code sepsis
was called.  Access was difficult to obtain - R groin line
placed.  Levophed was started.  2 unit PRBCs transfused.
.


Past Medical History:
1) Dyslipidimia
2) Hypertension
3) Type 2 DM
4) 01-18 echo: EF > 55%, mild pulmonary hypertension, 1+ MR
5) s/p L cornea implant 2 years ago

Social History:
Lives with 2 daughters, has 9 children total.  Had one
miscarriage.  Denies tobacco except for 6 month use as teen;
denies past or present EtOH, denies IVDU.  Has not worked
outside the home.


Family History:
no known miscarriages, no known Ca or liver disease

Physical Exam:
 T 98.4  HR 82 BP  165/106  RR 26  98%RA
Gen:  comfortable, lying flat on back, NAD
HEENT:  PERRL, anicteric, MMM, OP clear
Neck:  left IJ; supple no R LAD, no supraclavic LAD, JVP
nondistended
CV:  RRR, II/VI SEM
Resp:  CTAB
Abd:  obese, soft, NT, ND, +BS, liver palp 3cm below costal
margin, no splenomegaly
Ext:  no edema, 2+ DP pulses
Neuro:  A&Ox3, MAEW, sensation intact grossly

IMAGING:

RUQ U/S
FINDINGS: A small amount of sludge is observed in the
nondistended gallbladder. No stones are identified. The common
duct is not dilated. The liver shows no focal or textural
abnormalities. However, there is occlusion of the right portal
vein with visualized thrombus. Thrombus is also identified in
the left portal vein, however a small amount of flow is observed
around the thrombus. The pancreas is unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: Portal vein thrombus with occlusion of the right
portal and nonocclusive thrombus in the left portal vein.
.

The lungs are mildly hyperinflated. There is probable mild
cardiomegaly. The aortic knob is calcified and ectatic. No CHF
or effusion is identified. There is hazy opacity at the lung
base posteriorly on the lateral view. In the appropriate setting
this could represent an early pneumonic infiltrate. However, the
patient has demonstrated an opacity in this location on prior
chest x-ray examinations from 1991-03-31 and 2019-12-02.

IMPRESSION: Focal ill-defined opacity at the base posteriorly,
seen only on the lateral view. CT scan should be considered for
further characterization. Lungs are otherwise grossly clear,
without other evidence for infiltrate.
.
CT CHEST:

1. Portal vein occlusion with partial recanalization of the left
portal vein.

2. 2.5 x 2 cm left adrenal lesion, which is not fully
characterized on this exam and could represent an adenoma vs.
malignancy. Further characterization can be obtained with MRI.

3. No PE.

4. Mild pulmonary edema.

5. Multiple small hazy nodular opacities, which are nonspecific
in appearance. The largest nodule is located in the right upper
lobe and measures 5 mm. Given the presence of calcified lymph
nodes, the nodules could represent prior granulomatous disease
however neoplasm cannot be completely excluded. Follow-up exam
in six months is recommended.
.
MRCP:
1. Persistent complete right portal vein and partial left portal
vein thrombosis with small collateral formation. Large area of
edema within the left greater than right hepatic lobes with
heterogeneous perfusion is likely related to portal vein
thrombosis. No definite hepatic masses on this limited
evaluation.

2. No biliary source for infection. Normal caliber bile ducts.
Gallbladder wall edema likely relates to patient's anasarca.

3. 2.5 cm left adrenal lesion does not meet definitive criteria
for an adenoma. This could still represent an adenoma, but
metastasis or other primary adrenal lesion should be considered.

4. Asymmetric adnexa with right adnexa containing soft tissue
that may represent asymmetric ovarian tissue, however an ovarian
lesion cannot be excluded as this is only seen on one image set.
If clinically indicated, transvaginal ultrasound could be
performed to characterize further.
.
ERCP:
ERCP: 9 ERCP images were obtained by Dr. Blackwell, and
demonstrated normal biliary ducts without filling defect or
dilatation. Pancreatic duct was normal.
.
TV U/S:

Transabdominal imaging shows a uterus measuring 8.0 x 3.3 x 3.8
cm. Transvaginal imaging was performed to better evaluate the
uterus and the adnexa. There are multiple echogenic areas seen
in the periphery of the myometrium consistent with calcified
vessels. There is trace fluid seen within the endometrial
cavity. The endometrium itself is thin measuring 1-2 mm. Both
ovaries are normal. There is trace fluid in the pelvis. Neither
kidney demonstrates evidence of hydronephrosis.

IMPRESSION: Normal uterus and ovaries in a postmenopausal
patient.
.
COLONOSCOPY: See Callahan report.

Brief Hospital Course:
A/P:  72y/o F with sepsis, constitutional symptoms and weight
loss, and portal vein thrombosis.
.
1.  sepsis - unclear etiology of infection. WBC improved in Desert Regional Medical Center
on abx.  Ctx negative.  Abdominal exam benign and pt without abd
pain; biliary source of infection likely.  Pt with decent UOP,
mentating well, oxygenating well, lactate now normal, all
pointing away from poor end-organ perfusion.  A cortisol
stimulation test indicated normal adrenal function and she was
not started on steroids. Empiric coverage with amp/gent/flagyl
changed to imipenem, vanc, flagyl, azithro as pt lactate was
worsening on 02-06.  The lactate improved to 1.6. MVO2 76 prior to
d/c from Desert Regional Medical Center.  TSH and FT4 are WNL. Cdiff positive on flagyl.
The azithromycin was discontinued as there were no clear signs
of pneumonia.  She was continued on the imipenem and vancomycin
for empiric coverage.  She was weaned off of levofed and her
pressures remained stable.  A SPEP/UPEP were all normal.
Continued on imipenem, vanc, flagyl all started 02-06.  Switched
to levaquin on txf to floor and discharged to finish 3 more
days.
.
2.  portal vein thrombosis - unclear etiology - appears to be
somewhat chronic given recanalization, though has had a
decompensation of some sort with new bilirubinuria, jaundice.
Hepatitis serologies were all negative, and autoimmune panels
were also negative.  She had an essentially unremarkable MRCP
and was scheduled for an ERCP which was unremarkable as well.
Thought possibly 12-07 malignancy but colonoscopy not suggestive,
TV u/s not suggestive.  Factor V Leiden pending.  Homocysteine
normal.
.
4.  anemia - guaiac negative
- iron studies - in setting of blood transfusion, distorted
view, however pt appears to have a likely anemia of inflammation
- DIC labs- negative
- Given colonoscopy report, may have anemia 12-07 hemorrhage from
portal vein thrombosis.
.
5.  MFAT: Pt has been noted to have a wandering PCM intermittent
with MFAT over the past day.  Highest HR 120s.  Improved on
floor off pressors.
.
6.  metabolic acidosis - Likely due to lactic acidosis.  It
improved with hydration and was monitored.  Resolved on floor.
.
7.  diabetes mellitus - held metformin in setting of NPO and
lactic acidosis initially but then restarted metformin and pt.
had good FSG control
.
8. Kayla:  Cr at 0.8, at baseline; stayed at baseline.


Medications on Admission:
Lisinopril 20mg daily
Lipitor
Glucophage 500mg Wedowee Hospital
Predforte eye gtt
Afigan eye gtt

Discharge Medications:
1. Lisinopril 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

2. Atorvastatin 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day.

3. Metformin 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a
day).
4. Metronidazole 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO TID (3
times a day) for 3 days.
Disp:*9 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
5. Levofloxacin 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q24H (every
24 hours) for 3 days.
Disp:*3 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*


Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
Unit 5879 Box 9403
DPO AE 58944 VNA

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary
1. Portal Vein Thrombosis
2. Sepsis
3. Anemia
Secondary
1. Hypertension
2. Type II DM


Discharge Condition:
Good


Discharge Instructions:
You should follow up with Dr Blackwell in 1 week.  If you notice
any worsening CP, SOB, N/V, abd pain, headache, bleeding in your
stools, or pain on eating or having a bowel movement, please
return to the ER and/or contact your PCP.  Tamara Parks should take all
your medications as directed.  You should keep all your
appointments with health care providers

Followup Instructions:
You should call Dr. Blackwell at 316-978-9067 to schedule an
appointment
